speaker: interviewee
text: X-ray had film processing, but only for X-ray film. And so we'd take it to a local. First ones I got were when we got to Cham, we found a photographer who agreed to process our rolls of film. And I threatened him with death if he [LAUGHS] didn't-- if he tried to destroy any of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think I'm going to take you into that before we show the film.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't do it personally, but it was made evident that he was never to destroy that film.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever purchase a light [INAUDIBLE] when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, it's a pity.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never got into the-- from the time we moved into Cham, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everything happened so fast. This was in April. And then when we were in Cham, it was May. The war ended in May, and we were on our way back in--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we roll.

speaker: interviewer
text: Third tape with Walter Mason.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you took some photographs. Describe the process of which you took those photographs in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had film in my camera, and I decided to take those pictures. I didn't know how they'd come out until we'd actually reached Cham, where we found a photographer who agreed to process our film for us. And we were quite careful to intimate to him that there may be some dire consequences if those photographs didn't come out or the film was lost somehow or another. But they did come out, and I still have those photographs in my album.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did others take pictures as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Many of the members of our unit did take pictures. And when we published a unit history, we formed a pool where each of us submitted our pictures and then the editorial group selected which pictures they thought were typical and put it in the book itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that book put together?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1945 as the unit was being disbanded in the fall of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened from those hospitals in Cham? Where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then we had received word to go back to the United States. We turned all of our equipment and supplies over to a field hospital, which took over from us at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember boarding the ships?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let me, let me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get my question before or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. We can repeat it again. We are rolling again. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were saying-- back to your being shipped back to the United States. Can you describe that process for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I volunteered for a group that went with the advance party to the ship. And we went by truck and drove all the way back. The rest of the unit went by train. There were probably 20 or 30 of us. It's required, whenever they send them, that they have some medical first aid group to go along with the equipment. So I volunteered along with some others to go with the advance party and go by truck back to Le Havre, France, where we left again. And in that manner I was able to go through most of Germany and see some of the buildings that I hadn't seen and some of the cities that we hadn't seen. It's difficult to describe, but some areas were totally devastated and others, they were hardly touched. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was an interesting adventure [LAUGHS] to take that trek back, because along the roads were people going both ways, trying to find their homelands. In France we were cheered. In Germany they were just-- kind of had their heads down and heading one direction or another. A few of them that had been liberated, obviously, were giving us the 'hi' sign and waving to us in France, where they were enthusiastic. Of course, they were more enthusiastic when we were coming the other way. We didn't spend too much time in France coming-- going toward Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your trip back, did you ever assist any of these people you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Out, our convoy moved rather rapidly, so-- most of them were seeking some sort of a destination. Most of them were able to travel. None of them seemed to be in need of assistance, as far as that goes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When would you have-- what, what time was, of year was this, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in June.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you board ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember the exact day. It was the first part of July, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction coming back to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, exuberance. We were all glad to get back to the United States. And hopefully, although the war had not been completed in the far east, we were confident that it would-- now that we were able to devote our full efforts to that procedure that it wouldn't be that long. We were looking to the immediate goal, and that was with 30-day leave with our homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the 30-day leave, where did you have to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I reported back to Fort Sheridan, Illinois. And we were waiting. We knew we were going down to Camp Shelby-- or Camp-- it wasn't Shelby-- Camp Polk, Louisiana, where the unit was to regroup. It turned out when we got there it was not only just to regroup but to prepare for disbandment, because the war had ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: When where you discharged?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was discharged in August of 1946, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has your life gone since then? What have you done?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my first goal then was to return to college and complete my education. I did go back to the University of Michigan in 1946. And by virtue of the classes that I'd taken prior to that and some of the electives that I got at New York University under the Army's program, I was able to finish in June of 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your degree that you finished with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the degree you finished with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bachelor of science in pharmacy. And then I sought employment for-- and was able to do so immediately afterwards. I worked in retail pharmacy right after I graduated and eventually accepted a position with Parke-Davis and Company as a medical service and sales representative and lived in Dayton, Ohio for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your life proceed then? Into marriage and family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you want me to get into-- [LAUGHS] after I graduated from college, I had written to Aldean Nelson, who was a nurse in our unit. And she responded, and ended up we took a trip out to California. And she took a trip back to Michigan, and we were married in 1949 in April.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married in Dayton, Ohio. I had just taken that position with Parke-Davis and Company as a medical service and sales representative to represent the company in that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your life together living where?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were happy newlyweds. And then the, the Army intervened again. I had accepted a reserve commission in the Army after I graduated from college. And although I was not in the active reserve, the Korean conflict came up, and they deemed that we of the inactive reserve ought to have a little extra service. So we got called back to active duty to go to Korea. So we'd been married a little over two years when we had to force the idea of separation again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Korea?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went first to Baltimore, Maryland, where we went to Fort Meade. And my wife, Aldean, joined me there. It was required, I think, at that time that they give us some more training before sending us to Korea. But as soon as the minimum amount of training was done, we were on our way. We were able to enjoy some of the sights and sounds around the Baltimore area, Washington, DC, and Annapolis and Williamsburg and things of that nature. And then when we returned, we drove cross-country and my wife drove back to San Diego, where she stayed until I was able to return from Korea.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you return?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1952. July, I think it was, or August.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we have one son. He's 38 years old and lives in Tampa, Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kirk Mason.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you shared your experiences with Kirk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not until about a year ago was he ever interested, nor did I force. He began to get interested in our activities when he learned that we were working on a reunion for our unit, which occurred last September. And then he took our unit book and read it cover to cover and asked more questions about it. But for some reason or another, I never forced the information on him, nor did it really come up in any great sense to-- any urgency to impart that information to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've mentioned that you and-- your wife, Aldean, was a nurse in your unit.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: So therefore, you witnessed a lot together of these prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you talked through the years about what you've seen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we talk about it occasionally. It wasn't a subject that we dwelled upon a great deal. I think probably we talked more about it when we were talking about preparation for a reunion of the unit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that reunion of your unit, did you ever have trouble adapting to civilian life as a result of witnessing this result of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I think I probably have a keener perception of some values that I like to share other than, than any difficulties. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm more careful in making judgments. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm aware of other people's problems, I think, probably, than I would have been had I not gone through these. I don't think I needed any special psychological preparation, but the background, I think, helped me in the long run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you believe that your witnessing what you did changed any of your values or feelings towards religious practice?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I had any preconceived [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ideas of religion as-- per-- per se, but I think it gave me a deeper sense of man's attitude toward man, which I think is basically what religion is, and how we handle it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked about the feeling of not realizing until later on the full impact of the Holocaust. When do you believe that really hit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think probably within the last 10 years, although this has been an evolutionary project-- process as more information has been uncovered and more people were free to talk about it. We learn more about what was going on at the time that we were there than we had any inclination of at the time we were there. You have a rather narrow scope of things, and the big picture begins to fill in as you learn more about it. I find by listening to some educational programs what the United States position was and how much they knew about the Holocaust that we were never given any information of until years and years past that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you aware of the revisionists today who are trying to put this story as--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a fabric of imagination?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you want to say to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to talk to them. I, I feel anger that there is still that ignorance that they actually believe what they're talking about. How they can look at the evidence and say that that has been trumped up, it's difficult for me to perceive.

speaker: interviewer
text: The 120th Evacuation Hospital Unit had their reunion, you said, last year. What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a, a great sharing of experiences. Many of us hadn't seen each other for the 50 years. And it seemed like we picked up right where we left off. We shared many experiences that we weren't even aware of that the other was having at that time. So it was kind of an educational-- and it was a, a good feeling, period.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any stories about Buchenwald that were shared that you would like to share now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's so many different aspects. Some of them took more interest in the camp structure. And some of the Jewish members-- I saw a tape of one of the dentists that has since expired, and I was amazed at how much he had learned about the camp and its camp structure and how the differential uniforms were made for the different types of prisoners. And I felt kind of ashamed of myself that I didn't know more about it than he obviously took the trouble to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you were to talk to the future generations of tomorrow, which you are talking to by this taping, what do you want to tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think we shouldn't ignore the history. And I think by examining the facts that I think are now pretty well established, that we should learn something from that. That intolerance breeds a certain type of fascism or Nazism that could be disastrous to a whole generation. And we should be wary of those that might proceed along those lines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that we haven't covered that you would like to add at this moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't think of a thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Walt, we thank you for taking part in what you did, for saving lives, for being a part of this very special project.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to break and introduce your wife, Aldean, and then show some pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to have you meet my wife. This is Aldean Nelson Mason, the one to whom I referred earlier in our conversation. She was a nurse in our outfit. And although we didn't know each other real well during the unit, we did get to know each other later. And we were married in 1949. We've had 46 real good years together. Aldean, you shared a lot together in the Army. Do you feel that brings a closer bond to both of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's been very interesting. We've had much to talk about. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yes, I would say so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you notice that good-looking private?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you notice that good-looking private when you were together?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a sergeant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, a sergeant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I first noticed him in Tenby, Wales. And he worked in a dispensary. And he was just nice, Sergeant Mason. And then later on I saw, I saw him at different times in the outfit. When the nurses had to move, the enlisted men had to come over and take our [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] footlockers, which they didn't like. They-- it was the, their job and not the officers. But then I would notice him. But then in Cham, Germany I noticed him, too. And then when we returned to the States, when we were stationed at Camp Polk, Louisiana, I talked to him a little more before I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just do-- just one second. So, you know, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The last time I saw him was in the dental lab when we were having a checkup, and I met him there. And I was being discharged from the service, and he was going to have a wisdom tooth removed. And so I wished him luck into the civilian world when he was to be discharged. And that was it, never thought I'd ever see him again. And then I returned as a civilian to Washington state and on to California. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: We do have a tape also that we're doing of you, Aldean. And one question I have for both of you, were there other marriages from your 120th Evacuation Hospital Unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was. There was one other member of our unit that married a nurse-- trying to think of their name.

speaker: interviewee
text: On leave. Herb James.

speaker: interviewee
text: Herb James married another girl, another nurse. He married her before the unit actually disbanded so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we thank you both for doing what you did, again. And we're going to show some of your photos and the family photos now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is a picture of me taken in Muskegon, Michigan, I would say approximately 1927 to 1929. I was about five to seven years old at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The patch that you see there, the lamp and the sword crossed, was from the Army specialized training program that I attended at New York University. These were student soldiers sent back to college by the Army to proceed in professions that they felt might be most needy at the time. That picture was taken when I was home on furlough at approximately in December 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was taken in Muskegon, Michigan.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of lieutenant Aldean Nelson who later became my wife. This picture was taken approximately in 1943 or '44, I think somewhere on the west coast, either Washington or Oregon.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of me taken in Grand Rapids, Michigan in 1952, shortly after I'd come back from completing a tour of duty in Korea.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think what he did--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the popular style.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we are rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of my wife, Aldean, taken sometime in the mid '60s. We were at a convention at the Mackinac Island hotel, the Grand Hotel on Mackinac Island, I should say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Mackinac Island?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's between Lake Huron and Lake Superior. It's the Straits of Mackinac that separates the upper and lower peninsula of Michigan.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one second. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of my son taken when we were on vacation down in Florida last year.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would be 1994?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Son's name is Kirk Mason. He resides in Tampa at the present time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't handle that. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph I took along with some of the bodies of Sergeant Meppelink and Sergeant Fox standing by one of the typical wagons filled with the dead bodies. They were literally cross-piled, and several dozen bodies were on any one truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in the-- just as we entered the gates of Buchenwald, outside of the crematorium. These several carts were sitting there, just with all these bodies waiting to be burned.

speaker: interviewer
text: The month and year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in April 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph I took at Buchenwald concentration camp in April 1945, shortly after the liberation of the camp. General Patton has issued an edict that many of the cities of the nearby community of Weimar be brought into the camp to see the atrocities as we found them. There was a major who gave lectures to the community to explain to them exactly what we found, when, and where. And in some cases I tried to catch the reaction of the citizenry as they were told about these atrocities and as they were actually able to see them themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm rolling now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is another photo I took at Buchenwald concentration camp shortly after its liberation in April 1944 of the citizens being brought through to see the atrocities as we found them. They took the citizens in general, and there may be a German officer that we may have caught in there. And I'm not sure about that. But I tried to catch the expressions of the individuals as they viewed these atrocities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some of them are holding their noses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some are ordering their noses, and one is holding his throat. Some appear to be smiling, but I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a good photo. OK, we're on.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is another picture I took at the Buchenwald concentration camp in April 1945. These are some pictures of congressmen being viewed-- being brought in to view these atrocities. And in the foreground, I think you'll see a dark-jacketed lady there. It's Margaret Bourke-White, famous photographer who was taking pictures at the same time I was. This one gives you a little idea of the depth at which these bodies were piled on these carts and the large numbers that were piled on each cart.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is another photograph I took in Buchenwald concentration camp in April 1945. In addition to the bodies piled high and outside the courtyard to the crematorium, in the basement section there were a number of bodies just stacked like cordwood in a corner awaiting cremation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo taken of me in Cham, Germany in May of 1945. I'm standing outside the hospital where we had established a pharmacy, which serviced not only the patients in that particular hotel, but we had several hotels that we'd taken over. And the pharmacy services were provided from this particular location.
